coredata.dc:title: Unveiling pressure-sensitive adhesiveness of a carbonized polymer dot
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Saptarshi
Panigrahi, Harekrishna
Dinesh Kumar, K.
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: Dynamic market growth for water-borne Pressure Sensitive Adhesives (PSAs) calls forth new developments over conventional PSA. In order to achieve this, we made a trade-off between the polymerization and carbonization of acrylamide (A) and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (BA) without adding any additives to yield a series of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) by varying their mole ratio. Among the pyrolyzed materials, ABA4:1 outperformed others with its tacky adhesiveness in the probe tack test. The ABA4:1 exhibits noticeable blue fluorescence when irradiated with UV light that could be used for tracking the adhesive application area. Furthermore, ABA4:1 was found to generate a significant amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon irradiation with natural sunlight indicating its ability for self-sterilization. Thus a water-borne pressure-sensitive adhesive (PSA) was created through the partial carbonization of the substrates for better performance including a progressive touch of fluorescence and self-sterilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent adhesive
Carbon dot
Self-sterilization
Water-borne adhesive

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Probe tack test of ABA1:0, ABA4:1 and ABA3:2 unveiled the superiority of ABA4:1. (B) Digital images of fibrillation for ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2. (C) Lap shear test on the glass surface with ABA4:1. (D) Lap shear test on the aluminium surface with ABA4:1. (E) Storage modulus of ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2 with respect to angular frequency. (F) Tan δ of ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2 with respect to angular frequency.

(A) pXRD of ABA4:1 and (B) Raman spectra of ABA4:1 evidenced partial graphitization. (C) NMR spectra of ABA4:1 and (D) FTIR spectra of ABA4:1 divulged an abundance of functional groups on the surface. (E) FESEM image of ABA4:1 disclosed granular shape of ABA4:1 with size ∼18 nm.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of ABA4:1. (B) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of ABA4:1 showed typical excitation-dependent CPD-like spectra. (C) Digital fluorescent image of ABA4:1 that could be used for tracking the adhesive. (D) TGA curve of ABA4:1 unveiled its thermal stability. (E) An increase in fluorescence intensity of rhodamine 123 with respect to increasing irradiation time, marked the ROS generation potential of ABA4:1. (F) MTT assay results of ABA4:1 revealed negligible cytotoxicity of ABA4:1.
Synthesis of a fluorescent, self-sterilizable water-borne pressure-sensitive adhesive: ABA4:1 carbonized polymer dot.
Formulation of various acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide based CPDs.

row.entry: A mixture of hydrogel and viscous liquid; hence discarded
Precipitate at the bottom of the hydrothermal container; hence discarded

head.ce:title: Unveiling pressure-sensitive adhesiveness of a carbonized polymer dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna, 801103, Bihar, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna - 801103, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna, 801103, Bihar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna - 801103, Bihar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dynamic market growth for water-borne Pressure Sensitive Adhesives (PSAs) calls forth new developments over conventional PSA. In order to achieve this, we made a trade-off between the polymerization and carbonization of acrylamide (A) and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (BA) without adding any additives to yield a series of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) by varying their mole ratio. Among the pyrolyzed materials, ABA4:1 outperformed others with its tacky adhesiveness in the probe tack test. The ABA4:1 exhibits noticeable blue fluorescence when irradiated with UV light that could be used for tracking the adhesive application area. Furthermore, ABA4:1 was found to generate a significant amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon irradiation with natural sunlight indicating its ability for self-sterilization. Thus a water-borne pressure-sensitive adhesive (PSA) was created through the partial carbonization of the substrates for better performance including a progressive touch of fluorescence and self-sterilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Adhesives are one of the integral materials of human life ranging from common household, biomedical to industrial usage. However, different applications have different demands in terms of adhesiveness, mechanical strength, solubility, washability, etc. where the application environment too appears to be decisive. Likewise, fluorescent adhesives are desired in crafting or reconstructing artifacts, dental care, anti-counterfeits, etc. applications where tracking of the adhesive material is crucial [1–4]. Generally, such fluorescent adhesives are prepared by incorporating fluorescent dyes into the adhesive matrix [2,3,5–7]. This often encompasses additional conjugation chemistry to preclude the leaching of the added fluorescent dye. On the other hand, various government agencies are continuously accenting on limited usage of volatile organic compounds that invoke the need of designing waterborne fluorescent adhesives [8]. Earlier a CD incorporated waterborne fluorescent epoxy adhesive was reported but with additional conjugation chemistry for the assimilation of CD into a polymer matrix [4]. Nevertheless, a pressure-sensitive adhesive (PSA) is a viscoelastic material that is always tacky in the dry form at room temperature and adheres to different surfaces upon application of light contact pressure [9]. PSAs are generally used in tapes, labels, films, etc. with an industry vertical to packaging, automotive, healthcare, building & construction, and others. Promisingly, various market report studies delineate good market revenue with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 5.8% during 2021–2028 [10]. The USD 10.19 billion global markets is expected to reach USD 15.98 billion by the end of 2028. On a different note, a post COVID time will continually have an extra demand in the form of sterilizability. All these incite new developments in PSAs, especially a self-sterilizable PSA that could open up a new dynamism in this domain.

We frequently encounter a tacky adhesiveness in a few carbon dots (CDs) during their routine synthesis by a bottom-up approach, particularly in those which were synthesized from small aliphatic compounds prompting us to explore further their adhesive properties. It has already been emphasized that synthesis of CDs through ‘bottom-up’ approaches involve a critical balance between the extent of polymerization and carbonization was that evolved the concept of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [11]. A vast majority of the CDs obtained through the ‘bottom-up’ pyrolysis of polymerizable elements could be categorized as CPDs. Here, the nonselective random polymerization enables spatial extensions in all directions that are further crosslinked and carbonized with increased pyrolysis time. The precursors having easily cross-linkable active sites, dehydratable functional groups, unsaturated bonds aid in the formation of CPDs [12]. Structurally, they consist of a hydrophobic core with high cross-linking or slight graphitization surrounded by tangling polymeric chains. They are more stable than polymers due to their high cross-linking and carbonization while possessing good biocompatibility. Parallel to their predecessors, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CPDs are boosted with the merit of excellent photoluminescence, facile synthesis from cheap raw materials, decent stability (phot- and thermostable), light-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, etc. and have been explored for various optical and biomedical applications [13,14]. Nevertheless, acrylates are one of the typical materials used in PSA. Previously, few acrylamide-based CPDs were reported although for different applications (phosphorescence) [12,15]. Herein, we sought to explore a PSA from acrylamide-based CPDs. Additionally, we conjectured that the PSA will be self-sterilizable through visible light-mediated ROS generation.

Hydrothermal crosslinking and carbonization is an easy, green, inexpensive method for the synthesis of CDs including CPDs [16]. Along with the thermal treatment, it uses self-generated high pressure within a closed container that aids in CDs with superior photoluminescence, greater photo-stability, etc. as compared to other bottom-up methods [17,18]. Herein, we prepared a few acrylamide-based CPDs by hydrothermal treatment of acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide simply by varying their ratio and without adding any initiator such as potassium persulphate or azodiisobutyronitrile [12]. Their pressure-sensitive adhesiveness was evaluated by probe tack and lap shear method. Visible light-induced ROS generation and MTT assay were also performed for the CPD that exhibited the best PSA activity. In a nutshell, this report is a gracious exhibition of Nano-chemistry where the polymeric nature of a partially carbonized nano-material was taken into an advantage to unveil an unaccustomed but inexpensive PSA, boosted with fluorescent and self-sterilization properties without any additives (Scheme 1 ).

Acrylamide (electrophoresis grade), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (electrophoresis grade), dimethyl sulfoxide-d6, were bought from Merck, India. The dialysis membrane (benzoylated, MWCO 2 kDa), silicon wafer (single side polished, phosphorous dopant), and TEM grid (formvar/carbon supported copper grids, size 300 mesh) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India.

A total of 4 g of acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (as per Table 1 ) were taken in a 50 mL Teflon lined hydrothermal autoclave and 25 mL water was added to it. Then the autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the viscous liquid was dialyzed against water for 16 h. The retentate was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use. The same was repeated for all the cases; the only amount of acrylamide and bis-acrylamide was changed.

The powder X-ray diffraction (pXRD) analysis was performed at room temperature on a Rigaku TTRX-III diffractometer where Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) was used as an X-ray source. The dried sample was put on the sample holder and scanned over a range of 10°–70° with a scan step of 0.01313°.

The Raman spectra were obtained from a Raman microscope (Seki Tech, STR-750) using a He–Ne laser of wavelength 633 nm. The laser was focused onto the sample with an objective lens (NA = 0.45). The Raman scattered light was collected in backscattering geometry using a triple monochromator (Princeton Instruments).

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were acquired from a Hitachi S4800 FE-SEM. Dilute samples of the CPDs (water as diluent) were drop cast on silicon wafer chips and air-dried in vacuum. The samples were then gold-coated for 1 min before putting inside the SEM instrument for imaging. The images were further analyzed with ImageJ software.

The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was carried out at room temperature on a Beckman Coulter's Delsa nano submicron particle size analyzer (Brea, CA, USA). A dilute sample of the CPD (water as diluent) was taken in a transparent cuvette and the particle size was measured for 72 scans.
The 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker 400 MHz instrument using DMSO‑d 6 as the solvent with 32 scans.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from a PerkinElmer Spectrum 400 in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode with a scan number of 32. The dried samples were put on the holder and scanned over a wavenumber range of 4000–1000 cm−1.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The steady-state fluorescence spectroscopic measurements were performed on a Fluromax-4P spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). The water diluted sample was taken in a fluorescence cuvette at the spectra were recorded at room temperature.
The thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was done on a Q600 SDT (TA Instruments) in an N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in a range of 30 °C–800 °C.

The probe tack test of ABA1:0, ABA3:2, and ABA4:1 were carried out using a commercial stress-controlled rheometer (Modular compact rheometer, model-MCR302, Anton Paar, Austria) at ambient conditions (25 °C). A flat cylindrical steel probe (diameter of 8 mm) was mounted on the mobile arm of the device. The samples were kept on the steel fixture and the probe was brought into contact with the adhesive samples at a preselected rate of 0.1 mm/s. The motor was stopped when the preset force (F) = 2 N, was reached upon contact. After a pre-selected time, tcontact = 10 s, the probe was separated from the adhesive samples at a constant rate, vseparation = −0.1 mm/s. The force-displacement sensor measures the force as a function of time during bond formation and debonds separation.

The rheological properties of ABA1:0, ABA3:2, and ABA4:1 were studied using a commercial stress-controlled rheometer (Modular compact rheometer, model-MCR302, Anton Paar, Austria). The measurements were carried out in a parallel plate geometry with a plate diameter of 8 mm and a gap of 1 mm in between the two parallel plates. The frequency sweep experiments were performed from low to high oscillation frequencies (0.1–100 Hz) with a constant shear strain of 0.01% (selected from the linear viscoelastic region of the strain sweep curves) and at a constant temperature of 25 °C.

The aging studies of selected samples were conducted by measuring the lap shear strength of glass-glass joint and aluminium-aluminium joint after aging at 120 °C for 4 h in a hot air oven to understand the effect of aging on the heat resistance of the as-prepared adhesives.

The lap shear test of ABA4:1 was carried out using a universal testing machine (UTM, model- Zwick-Roell, Z010, Germany) at a crosshead speed of 1.3 mm/min at ambient conditions (25 °C). Two thoroughly cleaned substrates of size 100 mm × 25 mm × 2 mm were coated with adhesive and joined together under pressure. Lap joints with a lap area of 25 × 25 mm2 were prepared with aluminum and glass substrates. Adhesion was achieved using pressure applied by 3 passes of a 2 kg roller followed by a 24 h build-up time. The adhesive thickness of 0.05 mm was maintained for each sample. Five samples were tested for lap shear strength and the average was reported.

5 mg of ABA4:1 was dispersed in 2 mL of water. To this 2 μL of DHR 123 (1 μM) was added and kept under natural sunlight. The steady-state fluorescence spectra were taken at different time intervals at a fixed excitation wavelength of 490 nm and emission between 510 and 560 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted against different time intervals.

Human fibroblast cells at a cell density of 1 × 105 cells/well were seeded into a 24-well tissue culture plate. Different amounts of ABA4:1, along with positive and negative controls were added to respective wells and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Then 100 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated further for 4 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Thereafter, the medium was removed and washed with saline water twice. 200 μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve formazan crystals. The absorbance was measured at 562 nm on a plate reader and the values were plotted accordingly.

The CPDs were prepared by direct hydrothermal pyrolysis of varying amounts of acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide at 200 °C (Table 1). The high temperature and pressure for a prolonged time (12 h) inside a closed autoclave facilitates the polymerization and crosslinking of these small organic molecules that were followed by partial carbonization. As the ABA2:3 and ABA1:4 did not form uniform viscous liquids, they were discarded from further downstream studies. ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2 were then taken forward for probe tack test followed by characterization through a host of analytical techniques. Although exact structure predictability following carbonization is nil as with other CD researchers, the reproducibility here is very high.

The material that is sticky or tacky can easily be identified by touch. After perceiving the sticky nature of the ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2, we evaluated their tackiness through the probe tack test, which is a well-established method for investigation of adhesion properties of pressure-sensitive adhesives (PSAs) (Fig. 1 A) [19]. In general, in a tensile probe tack test, the maximum force peak during the early stage of debonding corresponds to the cavitation and the distinctive plateau observed during debonding corresponds to the fibrillation of adhesives [20,21]. ABA1:0 displayed very poor cavitation and fibrillation during the debonding (Fig. 1B). Whereas ABA3:2 showed good cavitation during the early stage of debonding, followed by very weak fibrillation. Only ABA4:1 displayed good cavitation during the early stage of debonding, followed by extensive fibrillation. This suggests that the adhesive properties of ABA4:1 are better when compared to ABA1:0 and ABA3:2. The tack energy (i.e., area under the force versus time curves of probe tack test) of ABA4:1 was significantly higher in comparison to ABA1:0 and ABA3:2. This suggests that the energy dissipated during the debonding process of ABA4:1 was significantly higher when compared to ABA1:0 and ABA3:2. Accordingly, ABA4:1 adhesive should show more resistance to separation during the debonding process of the probe tack test. Conspicuously, the ABA4:1 did not get detached from the probe or steel fixture even after 40 mm gapping (extended up to 400 s), the maximum capacity of the modular compact rheometer used in this study. The detachment occurred only after more than 10 test cycles up to 40 mm gaping for 400 s without any human touch intervention. In addition, the adhesion force did not drop drastically with test cycles. The maximum adhesion force fell very slowly from 3.34 N in test cycle 1–1.90 N in test cycle 12 (Fig. S1). This suggests that the repetitive test cycle did not deteriorate the adhesive behaviour of ABA4:1.

Furthermore, the adhesive strength of ABA4:1 was investigated by lap shear tests in glass and aluminium substrates (Fig. 1C and D). It was observed that the aluminium-aluminium joint showed marginally higher lap shear strength (0.13 MPa) when compared to glass-glass joint (0.12 MPa). This indicates the same magnitude of interactions of ABA4:1 with both aluminium and glass substrates. The lap shear strength of both glass-glass joint and aluminium-aluminium joint dropped considerably with test cycles (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). The maximum lap shear strength of glass-glass joint decreased very fast from 0.12 MPa in test cycle 1 to 0.04 MPa in test cycle 5 (Fig. S2). Similarly, the maximum lap shear strength of aluminium-aluminium joint decreased very fast from 0.13 MPa in test cycle 1 to 0.05 MPa in test cycle 5 (Fig. S3). The rheological studies were performed to understand the cohesive strength of the as-prepared adhesives (Fig. 1E and F). It was observed that in the entire frequency range, ABA4:1 showed higher storage modulus values and lower tan delta values when compared to ABA1:0 and ABA3:2, which is possibly due to the restriction in the polymer chain mobility within ABA4:1, indicating the higher cohesive strength of ABA4:1 when compared to ABA1:0 and ABA3:2 [22]. The above discussions elucidate that the ABA4:1 showed higher tack strength (extensive fibrillation in probe tack test) and cohesive strength (higher shear storage modulus and lower tan delta values) when compared to ABA3:2 and ABA1:0, which in turn lead to higher adhesive strength for ABA4:1.

The effect of aging on the lap shear strengths of ABA4:1 for glass-glass joint and aluminium-aluminium joint were also studied after aging at 120 °C for 4 h in a hot air oven. Interestingly, it was observed that aging did not significantly alter the adhesive strength of ABA4:1 for both glass-glass joint and aluminium-aluminium joint, which signifies that, the heat resistance of the as-prepared ABA4:1 is reasonably high.

After appraising such an unaccustomed aspect of hydrothermally pyrolyzed ABAs, we focussed on deciphering the usual CPD features of the ABA4:1 (See supplementary materials for basic characterization data of ABA1:0 and ABA3:2, Figs. S4–S10). The powder X-ray diffraction (pXRD) measurements of ABA4:1 disclosed wide and weak diffraction peaks at 27.5° and 41°, attributed to (002) and (100) planes of the graphitic framework respectively, whereas the peak broadness indicates the amorphous nature of ABA4:1 (Fig. 2 A) [23]. The Raman spectra of ABA4:1 revealed the G band at 1576 cm−1 corresponding to in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms, whereas the D band at 1363 cm−1 is ascribed to symmetry breaking at edges, defects, and the dangling terminal bonds (Fig. 2B) [24,25]. The proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy revealed an assortment of proton signals in the range of 7.0–9.0 ppm attributed to aromatic and amide protons (Fig. 2C) [26]. The extremely broad peak at 4.25 ± 1.0 ppm is accredited to alkene and amine protons. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of ABA4:1 divulged N–H stretching at 3400 cm−1, O–H stretching at 3050 cm−1, CO stretching at 1650 cm−1, and CC stretching at 1570 cm−1 (Fig. 2D) [12]. Thus, the 1H NMR and FTIR studies indicate an abundance of functional groups on ABA4:1 surface which is extremely crucial for the adhesiveness of a material. The field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) image of ABA4:1 unveiled granular structures of ABA4:1 with an average diameter of ∼18 nm. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) studies showed a number average diameter of 24.1 ± 3.8 nm (Fig. 2E, Figs. S11–S12).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of ABA4:1 disclosed various electronic transitions occurring within ABA4:1 (Fig. 3 A). The peak at 260 nm in Fig. 3A is ascribed to π-π* transitions, whereas the shoulder at 310 nm which stretched towards a higher wavelength is attributed to the various n-π* transitions of surface functional groups. The steady-state fluorescence spectra of ABA4:1 showed typical CPD-like excitation-dependent fluorescence emission in the blue region of the visible light spectrum (Fig. 3B) [11,12]. Rather importantly, this denotes the creation of a fluorescent PSA without using any fluorescent additives. This intrinsic fluorescence of ABA4:1 could be used to the additional advantage of tracking the adhesive application area (Fig. 3C). Concisely, all the above experimentations collectively establish ABA4:1 as a CPD.

The thermal stability of ABA4:1 was tested by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) which revealed a four-step decomposition profile (Fig. 3D) [27]. The initial 11.78% weight loss up to 150 °C is ascribed to the elimination of water molecules adsorbed by the ABA4:1. The second 29.12% weight loss up to 250 °C is accredited to the onset of decomposition of dangling terminal moieties. The third weight loss of 32.73% up to 380 °C is attributed to the complete decomposition of surface moieties. The final fourth weight loss of 25.76% is imputed to the decomposition of the carbonized core.

The ability to generate ROS by ABA4:1 under natural sunlight was examined by using dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR 123) as a probe [28]. The non-fluorescent DHR 123 got oxidized by ROS generated from ABA4:1 to green fluorescent rhodamine 123, which was measured with fluorescence spectroscopy. The sharp amplification in green fluorescent intensity of rhodamine 123 with increased irradiation time signifies a decent ROS generation capacity of ABA4:1 (Fig. 3E). Such ROS generation from CDs has been reported to annihilate microbes [29]. Most excitingly, this denotes the self-sterilizing prospect of ABA4:1 under visible light. Thus, a self-sterilizing PSA has been created that eliminates the need of going through harsh sterilization procedures such as autoclaving, gamma or UV-light irradiation, etc., a first of its kind developed to the best of our knowledge. MTT assay with fibroblast cell line was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of ABA4:1 [30]. Decent cell viability was observed in presence of ABA4:1 up to 100 mg signifying good biocompatibility of ABA4:1 (Fig. 3F). Suggestively, ABA4:1 could be used for medical purposes, most importantly eliminating the need for separate sterilization. Although, a comprehensive examination of the nanotoxicity of ABA4:1 is obligatory before considering it as a medical adhesive.

Thus, a novel acrylamide-based water-borne, fluorescent PSA was generated simply by hydrothermal pyrolysis of a judicious amount of acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide. When we focused to comprehend the reason behind the superior PSA property of ABA4:1, we noted that the fibrillation and the shear storage modulus of ABA4:1 were found to be optimum when compared to ABA3:2 and ABA1:0, which in turn lead to higher adhesive strength for ABA4:1. As we compared the surface functionalities of ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2 through FTIR and NMR spectroscopy (Figs. S5 and S6), we found some differences in the regions that correspond to amines, hydroxyls, alkenes, and methylenes. It is well known that the functional groups present in the adhesive will also play a crucial role in dictating the adhesive strength (interacting with the substrate) [31]. However, here, the differences in the number of functional groups present in ABA1:0, ABA4:1, and ABA3:2 might not be very significant to have a prominent effect on the adhesive strength. Therefore, it appears that the role of tack strength and cohesive strength in influencing the adhesive strength seems to be more prominent in comparison to the role of functional groups present in the adhesives.

Traditional acrylamide-based adhesives have found important uses in packaging, labels, automotive, and shoe industries. However, non-trackability, cytotoxicity, and other limitations have restricted the broad use of this class of adhesives including biomedical applications. Herein, we are able to demonstrate that through a simple carbonization process with an optimized substrate ratio, we could get more versatility out of this adhesive. This value addition is cheap and has the potential for scale-up production. The mole ratio of acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide was adjusted to crosslink and pyrolyze in a hydrothermal autoclave without any additives. The adhesive nature of the as-prepared CPDs was evaluated through a probe tack test and a lap shear test, where the performance ABA4:1 adhesive was found to be superior when compared to other CPDs. Conventional CPD-like properties of ABA4:1 were appraised through pXRD, Raman spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, etc. The blue fluorescence of ABA4:1 could easily be tracked through the naked eye under UV-light irradiation. ROS generation capacity of ABA4:1 under visible light points toward its self-sterilization potential that could broaden the horizon of its applicability including its use as medical adhesives. Overall, an acrylamide-based CPD was developed exclusively to discover a novel water-borne, fluorescence, and self-sterilizable PSA.

Saptarshi Mandal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Harekrishna Panigrahi: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. K. Dinesh Kumar: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Prolay Das: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.D. and K.D.K. thank the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Patna for infrastructural and financial assistance. S.M. and H.P. thank IIT Patna for Institute Research Fellowship.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2022.125102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance water-borne fluorescent acrylic-based adhesive: synthesis and application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Upadhyaya, A. Konwar, D. Chowdhury, N. Sen Sarma, High-performance water-borne fluorescent acrylic-based adhesive: synthesis and application, RSC Adv. 10 (2020) 25408-25417.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Usefulness of orthodontic adhesive-containing fluorescent dye

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Namura, T. Tsuruoka, C. Ryu, M. Kaketani, N. Shimizu, Usefulness of orthodontic adhesive-containing fluorescent dye, Eur. J. Orthod. 32 (2010) 620-626.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removable/fluorescent adhesive made by melamine-formaldehyde cross-linked polyvinyl alcohol and its repair application in artifacts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, L. He, A. Pan, Y. Wu, Removable/fluorescent adhesive made by melamine-formaldehyde cross-linked polyvinyl alcohol and its repair application in artifacts, Appl. Surf. Sci. 495 (2019) 143570.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots-ornamented waterborne epoxy-based fluorescent adhesive via reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer-mediated miniemulsion polymerization: a potential material for art conservation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Samanta, S.L. Banerjee, K. Bhattacharya, N.K. Singha, Graphene Quantum Dots-Ornamented Waterborne Epoxy-Based Fluorescent Adhesive via Reversible Addition-Fragmentation Chain Transfer-Mediated Miniemulsion Polymerization: A Potential Material for Art Conservation, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 13 (2021) 36307-36319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of fluorescent dye on mechanical properties of adhesive systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.T.P. Araujo, L.T. Prieto, D.C.R.S. Oliveira, E.K. Coppini, A.F. Lima, W.J. Silva, L.A.M.S. Paulillo, Influence of fluorescent dye on mechanical properties of adhesive systems, Int. J. Adhes. Adhes. 47 (2013) 129-133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on a novel fluorescent anti-counterfeiting acrylate pressure-sensitive adhesive

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, Z. Zhou, X. Yu, K. Zhang, Q. Chen, M. Hong, H. Fu, Study on a novel fluorescent anti-counterfeiting acrylate pressure-sensitive adhesive, J. Adhes. (2021) 1-16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV fluorescent epoxy adhesives from noncovalent and covalent incorporation of coumarin dyes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.D. McFadden, K. Frederick, L.A. Arguello, Y. Zhang, P. Vandiver, N. Odegaard, D.A. Loy, UV Fluorescent Epoxy Adhesives from Noncovalent and Covalent Incorporation of Coumarin Dyes, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 9 (2017) 10061-10068.
S. Pradhan, Waterborne Adhesives, in: Green Adhes., Wiley Online Library, 2020: pp. 121-128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development trends in pressure-sensitive adhesive systems
